### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **5 V's of Big Data** in detail with **real-time Industry 4.0 examples** for each V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **(i) Volume (1.5 marks):** Refers to the size of data. In Industry 4.0, a single modern connected car generates roughly **4 TB of data per day** from cameras, LiDAR, and telematics. A smart factory with 1000 sensors easily crosses the **Petabyte/year** threshold. This volume exceeds the capacity of a single RDBMS server.
>
> **(ii) Velocity (1.5 marks):** The speed at which data is generated and must be processed. Industrial examples:
> - Vibration sensor on a CNC spindle: **10 kHz sampling rate**.
> - Smart grid frequency sensor: must be processed in **< 16 ms** (one AC cycle at 50/60 Hz).
> - High-frequency trading in Industry 4.0 B2B platforms: **microsecond latency**.
>
> **(iii) Variety (1 mark):** Different data formats.
> - **Structured:** PLC tag database tables.
> - **Semi-structured:** OPC-UA JSON from MES.
> - **Unstructured:** Thermal images, .wav acoustic files, maintenance work-order PDFs.
>
> **(iv) Veracity (1 mark):** Trustworthiness and quality. Industrial sensor noise, calibration drift, and missing data can lead to **false alarms** in predictive maintenance. Therefore, data cleaning and statistical outlier detection (e.g., Z-score, IQR) are essential.
>
> **(v) Value (1 mark):** The ultimate business worth extracted. A 5% improvement in Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) in a smart factory can yield **millions in annual savings**, justifying the Big Data investment.
>
> **(vi) Conclusion (1 mark):** The 5 V's are not isolated; they are *inter-dependent design constraints*. Addressing volume demands distributed storage; addressing velocity demands stream processing; addressing variety demands data lakes; addressing veracity demands cleaning pipelines; addressing value demands ML.

---

**(b)** A smart factory has **200 robotic arms**. Each arm has **6 sensors** (torque, position, current, vibration, temperature, encoder) sampling at **2 kHz**, with each sample being **8 bytes**. The factory operates **20 hours/day, 350 days/year**. Calculate the **annual data volume in Terabytes (TB)**. State whether this qualifies as Big Data. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Sampling rate per arm (1 mark):**
> $n_{\text{arm}} = 6 \times 2000 = 12{,}000 \text{ samples/sec}$
>
> **Step 2 — Bytes per second per arm (1 mark):**
> $R_{\text{arm}} = 12{,}000 \times 8 = 96{,}000 \text{ Bytes/sec}$
>
> **Step 3 — Factory-wide bytes per second (1 mark):**
> $R_{\text{factory}} = 96{,}000 \times 200 = 19{,}200{,}000 \text{ Bytes/sec} = 1.92 \times 10^7 \text{ B/s}$
>
> **Step 4 — Operating seconds per day (1 mark):**
> $T_{\text{day}} = 20 \times 3600 = 72{,}000 \text{ sec/day}$
>
> **Step 5 — Daily data volume (1 mark):**
> $D_{\text{daily}} = 1.92 \times 10^7 \times 72{,}000 = 1.3824 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bytes/day}$
>
> **Step 6 — Annual data volume (1 mark):**
> $D_{\text{annual}} = 1.3824 \times 10^{12} \times 350 = 4.8384 \times 10^{14} \text{ Bytes/yr}$
>
> **Step 7 — Convert to Terabytes (1 mark):**
> $1 \text{ TB} = 2^{40} = 1.0995 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bytes}$
> $D_{\text{TB}} = \dfrac{4.8384 \times 10^{14}}{1.0995 \times 10^{12}} \approx 440 \text{ TB/yr}$
>
> **Conclusion:** $\boxed{D_{\text{annual}} \approx 440 \text{ TB/year}}$, which **definitely qualifies as Big Data** (well beyond a single-server RDBMS threshold) and warrants a distributed Hadoop/Spark stack.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **Lambda Architecture** for Big Data processing. List its **two main layers** and explain how the **Serving Layer** merges their outputs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the **Mermaid Lambda Architecture diagram in Section 4.3** of these notes. The student should draw three labelled boxes: Batch Layer, Speed Layer, Serving Layer, with arrows from a "Data Source" box into both Batch and Speed layers, and arrows from both converging into the Serving Layer, finally pointing to a "Query" or "Application" box.
>
> **Batch Layer (1.5 marks):**
> - Stores the **master, immutable raw dataset** in HDFS.
> - Runs periodic (hourly/daily) **MapReduce or Spark batch jobs** to compute comprehensive views.
> - Provides **high-accuracy, high-latency** answers.
>
> **Speed Layer (1.5 marks):**
> - Processes **real-time streaming data** (e.g., Apache Flink, Storm).
> - Maintains **incremental, in-memory** updates.
> - Provides **low-latency, approximate** answers.
>
> **Serving Layer (1 mark):**
> - Uses **NoSQL stores** like HBase or Cassandra.
> - Merges the **batch views** (accurate but stale) and the **real-time views** (fresh but approximate) at query time using the formula:
> $ \text{Query\_Result} = \text{Batch\_View} \oplus \text{Realtime\_View} $
> where $\oplus$ denotes an **additive merge** (e.g., for counts) or a **replacement merge** (e.g., for latest state).

---

**(b)** Compare **Traditional Data Processing** and **Big Data Processing** under the heads: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Storage Architecture, Processing Model, and Example Use Case.** Why is RDBMS insufficient for Industry 4.0 workloads? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Comparison Table (4 marks — ½ mark per cell filled, take any 8 cells):**
>
> | Dimension | Traditional Data | Big Data |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Volume | GB | PB to ZB |
> | Velocity | Daily batch | Real-time streams |
> | Variety | Structured only | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
> | Storage | Single server, centralised | Distributed cluster (HDFS) |
> | Processing | Sequential SQL queries | Parallel MapReduce / Spark |
> | Schema | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read |
> | Example | Nightly ERP report | Real-time predictive maintenance |
> | Hardware | Vertical scaling (SMP) | Horizontal scaling (commodity) |
>
> **Why RDBMS is insufficient (3 marks):**
> 1. **Vertical scaling ceiling:** RDBMS relies on scaling up a single server, but there is a hardware limit (CPU, RAM, disk). Modern I4.0 workloads demand **PB-scale** storage that no single server can host.
> 2. **Schema rigidity:** RDBMS enforces a fixed schema-on-write, but industrial IoT data is heterogeneous (vibration waveforms, JSON, video) and **schema-less or evolving**.
> 3. **No native streaming:** RDBMS is fundamentally **batch-oriented**; it cannot natively process continuous sensor streams at **kHz sampling rates**.
> 4. **No unstructured support:** Storing image/video blobs in a BLOB column is inefficient; RDBMS lacks the parallel processing primitives for computer-vision analytics.
> 5. **Single point of failure:** A monolithic RDBMS becomes a mission-critical bottleneck; HDFS offers **3x replication by default**.
>
> Hence, distributed Big Data stacks (Hadoop + Spark + NoSQL) are mandatory for Industry 4.0.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Big Data" with "Lots of Data."** Big Data is a *paradigm* — it is defined by the 5 V's, not just volume. A 500 GB structured SQL table is NOT Big Data; a 5 TB mixed-format IIoT stream IS.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Trap:** When converting to PB/TB, use **binary** ($2^{10}$), not decimal ($10^3$). KTU expects $1 \text{ TB} = 2^{40}$ Bytes for storage questions.
> 3. **Forgetting Operating Hours:** A frequent mistake in the sensor data calculation is to assume **24/7** operation. Always read the question and multiply only by *actual* operating hours and days.
> 4. **Diagrams must be hand-drawable:** In the offline KTU paper, Mermaid cannot be used. Students must draw the Lambda Architecture with **three labelled rectangles** and **arrows** between them — labelled with "HDFS," "Spark," "Flink," and "HBase."
> 5. **No marks for definitions without examples:** When asked about the 5 V's, every V must be paired with a **concrete Industry 4.0 example** (e.g., vibration sensor for Velocity, OPC-UA JSON for Variety).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Big Data Definition:** Large, fast, varied, trustworthy, and valuable data that exceeds the capability of traditional RDBMS.
- **The 5 V's:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- **Data Volume Units (Binary):** 1 KB = $2^{10}$ B, 1 MB = $2^{20}$ B, 1 GB = $2^{30}$ B, 1 TB = $2^{40}$ B, 1 PB = $2^{50}$ B, 1 EB = $2^{60}$ B, 1 ZB = $2^{70}$ B.
- **Data Variety Classes:** Structured (SQL tables) → Semi-structured (JSON/XML) → Unstructured (image/audio/video).
- **Annual Sensor Data Formula:** $D_{\text{annual}} = K \times f_s \times B_s \times M \times H \times 3600 \times D$ Bytes/year.
- **HDFS Replication Budget:** $B = D_{total} \times 3$ for default 3× replication.
- **Big Data Stack Components:**
  - Storage → **HDFS, S3, GFS**
  - Ingestion → **Apache Kafka, Flume**
  - Batch Processing → **Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Spark**
  - Stream Processing → **Apache Flink, Storm, Spark Streaming**
  - Serving → **HBase, Cassandra, Elasticsearch**
  - Analytics → **MLlib, TensorFlow, PySpark**
  - Visualisation → **Grafana, Power BI, Tableau**
- **Lambda Architecture:** Batch Layer (accuracy) + Speed Layer (freshness) → Serving Layer (merged query response).
- **Why RDBMS fails in I4.0:** Vertical scaling ceiling, rigid schema, no native streaming, no unstructured support, single point of failure.
- **KTU-Expected Keywords in Answers:** "horizontal scaling," "distributed storage," "schema-on-read," "data lake," "real-time stream processing," "Industry 4.0 cyber-physical systems."
- **Exam Tip:** Always quote **at least one Industry 4.0 example** per V to score full marks. A definition without an example will cost you **0.5 to 1 mark** per V.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET862 - INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Characteristics** refer to the defining attributes that distinguish massive, complex, and rapidly evolving datasets from traditional structured data. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Industry 4.0 syllabus, the characteristics are universally framed around the **"5 V's Model"** — **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value** — which collectively describe the scale, speed, diversity, trustworthiness, and actionable worth of data in modern cyber-physical systems.

In the context of **Industry 4.0**, Big Data is generated from heterogeneous sources such as **Internet of Things (IoT) sensors**, **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, **SCADA systems**, **Machine-to-Machine (M2M) communication**, **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** platforms, and **social media feeds**. The term "Big Data" therefore refers to datasets whose size, speed, or complexity exceeds the capability of conventional relational database management systems (RDBMS) to capture, store, manage, and analyze within tolerable elapsed time.

The standard quantitative threshold often used in industry is the **"Petabyte Threshold"**, where data scales beyond **1 Petabyte (10¹⁵ bytes)** and approaches **Zettabyte (10²¹ bytes)** or **Yottabyte (10²⁴ bytes)** levels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Library Analogy:**
> Imagine a small neighbourhood library with 5,000 books (Traditional Data). Every book is neatly catalogued by author, genre, and year. The librarian knows exactly where each book sits.
>
> Now imagine a **nationwide digital archive** that receives **10 million new documents every minute** from CCTV cameras, satellites, smartphones, ATM transactions, and factory sensors (Big Data). The documents arrive in **multiple languages** (text, video, audio, sensor signals), some are **duplicates**, some are **corrupted**, and the librarian must still find a useful pattern — say, "which factory is producing defective parts" — within minutes.
>
> This is exactly the challenge of Big Data: it is not just *more* data, it is **structurally different** data requiring new tools, architectures (like Hadoop and Spark), and analytical methods (like Machine Learning and Stream Processing).

## 1.3 Why Big Data Characteristics Matter in Industry 4.0

- **Smart Manufacturing** decisions require **real-time processing** of terabytes of sensor data per shift.
- **Predictive Maintenance** depends on **high-velocity** data from vibration, temperature, and acoustic sensors.
- **Supply Chain 4.0** integration requires merging **structured (ERP)** and **unstructured (social media, weather)** datasets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome mapping expects students to *identify, classify, and explain* each of the 5 V's with at least one real-time Industry 4.0 example per V.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 V's of Big Data — Structured Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Volume (Scale of Data)

**Definition:** Volume refers to the **sheer magnitude** of data generated and stored. It is the most intuitive characteristic.

- Measured in **Megabytes (MB) → Gigabytes (GB) → Terabytes (TB) → Petabytes (PB) → Exabytes (EB) → Zettabytes (ZB) → Yottabytes (YB)**.
- IDC's *Global Datasphere* predicts global data creation will exceed **175 Zettabytes by 2025**.
- Industry 4.0 sources: A single **Boeing 787** generates **~5 TB of data per flight**; a **connected car** generates **~4 TB per day**; a **smart factory** can generate **~1 PB per day**.

**Why it matters:** Traditional RDBMS systems (e.g., MySQL, Oracle) cannot scale horizontally to handle this volume; hence distributed storage like **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is used.

### 2.1.2 Velocity (Speed of Generation & Processing)

**Definition:** Velocity describes the **rate at which data is produced, captured, and must be processed** to be useful.

- Two sub-types:
  - **Batch Velocity** — data accumulated and processed periodically (e.g., daily sales reports).
  - **Real-Time / Stream Velocity** — data processed within **milliseconds to seconds** (e.g., high-frequency stock trading, autonomous vehicle obstacle detection).
- Measured in **events per second (EPS)**, **messages per second (MPS)**, or **GB/hour**.

**How it works:** Technologies like **Apache Kafka**, **Apache Flink**, and **Amazon Kinesis** handle high-velocity streaming data using **event-driven architectures**.

### 2.1.3 Variety (Diversity of Data Types)

**Definition:** Variety refers to the **heterogeneity of data types and sources**.

| Sub-Type | Description | Industry 4.0 Example |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Structured** | Tabular, schema-defined (rows & columns) | ERP transaction records, SQL databases |
| **Semi-Structured** | Partial schema (tags, metadata) | JSON logs, XML sensor packets, emails |
| **Unstructured** | No predefined schema (80% of Big Data) | CCTV footage, vibration waveforms, operator voice notes |
| **Quasi-Structured** | Textual with irregular format | Web clickstreams, server logs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Approximately **80–90% of all enterprise data is unstructured**, and Industry 4.0 amplifies this through IoT sensor diversity.

### 2.1.4 Veracity (Trustworthiness & Quality)

**Definition:** Veracity addresses the **accuracy, reliability, and trustworthiness** of data.

- Data can be **noisy**, **incomplete**, **biased**, or **corrupted** due to sensor malfunctions, transmission errors, or adversarial attacks.
- Measured through metrics like **data completeness ratio**, **accuracy percentage**, and **noise-to-signal ratio**.

$$
\text{Data Quality Score} = \frac{\text{Correct Records}}{\text{Total Records}} \times 100\%
$$

**Why it matters:** In Industry 4.0, a faulty temperature sensor feeding **false data** into a predictive maintenance system can cause **catastrophic machine failure** or unnecessary downtime costing millions.

### 2.1.5 Value (Actionable Insight)

**Definition:** Value represents the **business, operational, or strategic worth** extracted from Big Data after processing.

- Data alone has **low intrinsic value**; value emerges after **analytics, correlation, and pattern recognition**.
- Implementation example: Transforming raw vibration data → failure prediction → **15% reduction in maintenance costs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Value Pyramid" Concept:** Raw Data → Information (structured) → Knowledge (patterns) → Wisdom (actionable decisions). Big Data analytics aims to climb this pyramid.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Metric** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|-------------|----------------------|----------|-----------------|
| Volume Scaling | $1 \text{ PB} = 10^{15} \text{ Bytes}$ | Bytes | Storage capacity planning |
| Velocity | $V = \dfrac{\text{Events Processed}}{\text{Time Interval}}$ | EPS (events/sec) | Throughput measurement |
| Data Quality | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Valid Records}}{\text{Total Records}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Veracity assessment |
| Compression Ratio | $C_R = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless | Storage optimization |
| Hadoop Replication | $\text{Total Storage} = \text{Data Size} \times \text{Replication Factor (default 3)}$ | Bytes | HDFS fault-tolerance |
| Real-time Latency | $L = T_{\text{ingestion}} + T_{\text{processing}} + T_{\text{output}}$ | Milliseconds (ms) | Velocity SLA |
| Value Realization | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Gain from Insight} - \text{Analytics Cost}}{\text{Analytics Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Business value of analytics |
| Storage Growth (Moore's Law for Data) | $D(t) = D_0 \times 2^{t/n}$ where $n$ = doubling period (years) | Bytes | Capacity forecasting |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** Veracity + Volume — millions of smart meters generate noisy data requiring analytics for load balancing.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Velocity + Variety — LIDAR, radar, and camera data must be processed in **< 100 ms** for collision avoidance.
- **Digital Twins:** All 5 V's combined — a digital twin of a factory integrates multi-source data to simulate, predict, and optimize operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Storage Requirements for Industry 4.0 Sensor Network

**Problem Statement:**
A smart manufacturing plant deploys **2,000 IoT sensors**. Each sensor generates **1 KB of data per second** operating **24 hours a day, 7 days a week**. The HDFS cluster uses a **replication factor of 3** for fault tolerance. Estimate the **total daily storage requirement** in Terabytes (TB).

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate Data Generated Per Second by the Entire Network**

$$
\text{Total Rate} = N_{\text{sensors}} \times R_{\text{per sensor}}
$$

$$
\text{Total Rate} = 2{,}000 \times 1 \text{ KB/s} = 2{,}000 \text{ KB/s} = 2{,}000 \times 1{,}024 \text{ Bytes/s} = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** With 2,000 sensors each pushing 1 KB every second, the aggregate ingestion rate is 2,000 KB per second. This is the **velocity** of the data stream.

**Step 2: Calculate Data Generated Per Day (24 Hours)**

$$
\text{Seconds per Day} = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\text{Daily Raw Data} = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{Daily Raw Data} = 176{,}947{,}200{,}000 \text{ Bytes} \approx 176.95 \text{ GB}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** In one day, the network produces roughly 177 GB of raw sensor data. This is the **volume** of the Big Data characteristic.

**Step 3: Apply HDFS Replication Factor of 3**

$$
\text{Actual Storage} = \text{Daily Raw Data} \times \text{Replication Factor}
$$

$$
\text{Actual Storage} = 176.95 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 530.85 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** Hadoop's default replication ensures fault tolerance by storing 3 copies across different nodes. This triples the storage requirement.

**Step 4: Convert to Terabytes (TB)**

$$
1 \text{ TB} = 1{,}024 \text{ GB} = 1{,}099{,}511{,}627{,}776 \text{ Bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Storage in TB} = \dfrac{530.85 \text{ GB}}{1{,}024 \text{ GB/TB}} \approx 0.5184 \text{ TB/day}
$$

**Step 5: Annual Storage Projection**

$$
\text{Annual Storage} = 0.5184 \text{ TB/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \approx 189.2 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** A modest plant with only 2,000 sensors consumes nearly **200 TB per year** — quickly illustrating why **traditional RDBMS fails** and **distributed storage becomes mandatory** in Industry 4.0.

## 3.2 Derivation: Real-Time Processing Latency for Quality Control

**Problem Statement:**
A bottling plant uses a vision system that captures **120 frames per second** at **2 MB per frame**. Determine the **maximum allowable processing latency per frame** to maintain real-time quality control, and the **total bandwidth requirement**.

**Step 1: Maximum Latency per Frame**

$$
L_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Frame Rate}} = \dfrac{1}{120} \text{ seconds} = 8.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Bandwidth Requirement**

$$
\text{Bandwidth} = \text{Frame Rate} \times \text{Frame Size}
$$

$$
\text{Bandwidth} = 120 \text{ frames/s} \times 2 \text{ MB/frame} = 240 \text{ MB/s} = 1.92 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** The vision system must complete inference within **8.33 ms** per frame, demanding **edge computing** or **GPU-accelerated processing** — clearly demonstrating the **Velocity** characteristic of Big Data.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 5 V's Mapped to Industry 4.0 Use Cases

| **Big Data Characteristic** | **Industrial KPI** | **Industry 4.0 Use Case** | **Tool / Technology** |
|------------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| **Volume** | TB / PB per day | Smart factory sensor logs | HDFS, Amazon S3 |
| **Velocity** | ms-level response | Autonomous AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) navigation | Apache Kafka, Flink |
| **Variety** | Format diversity | Multi-modal quality inspection (visual + thermal + acoustic) | NoSQL (MongoDB), Data Lake |
| **Veracity** | Data quality score (%) | Sensor fault detection in CNC machines | Anomaly detection algorithms |
| **Value** | Cost savings / OEE improvement | Predictive maintenance ROI | Machine Learning, Digital Twin |

> [!NOTE]
> **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** = Availability × Performance × Quality. Big Data analytics directly targets each OEE component, making it a strategic lever in Industry 4.0.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**List and briefly explain any three characteristics of Big Data with Industry 4.0 examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three most important characteristics of Big Data are:

1. **Volume (1 mark):** Refers to the massive scale of data generated. Example: A connected car generates approximately **4 TB of data per day** from GPS, cameras, and engine sensors.

2. **Velocity (1 mark):** Refers to the speed at which data is generated and processed. Example: A vision inspection system on a production line must process **120 frames/second** for real-time defect detection.

3. **Variety (1 mark):** Refers to the diverse data types — structured, semi-structured, and unstructured. Example: A smart factory combines structured ERP data, semi-structured JSON sensor logs, and unstructured video footage from surveillance cameras.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between structured and unstructured data. Give one Industry 4.0 example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Structured Data** | **Unstructured Data** |
|------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Data with a predefined schema organized in rows and columns | Data without a predefined schema or format |
| **Storage** | Relational databases (SQL, Oracle) | Data Lakes, NoSQL (MongoDB) |
| **Industry 4.0 Example (1 mark)** | ERP production records with fields like timestamp, machine ID, output count | CCTV footage from shop floor surveillance or audio recordings of operator feedback |

Structured data accounts for only **~10–20%** of enterprise data, while unstructured constitutes **~80–90%**, making variety a critical Big Data characteristic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**a)** Explain in detail the **5 V's of Big Data** with suitable Industry 4.0 examples. **(7 marks)**
**b)** Discuss the **challenges** in handling Big Data characteristics in a smart manufacturing environment and suggest suitable technologies to overcome them. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A:

#### Part (a) — 5 V's of Big Data with Industry 4.0 Examples (7 marks)

**1. Volume (1.4 marks):**
Volume refers to the **sheer size and scale** of data. In Industry 4.0, a Boeing 787 aircraft generates approximately **5 TB of data per flight**, and a smart factory with 10,000 sensors can produce **petabytes annually**. Traditional storage is inadequate; hence **distributed file systems** are deployed.

**2. Velocity (1.4 marks):**
Velocity denotes the **speed of data generation and processing**. In a bottling plant, a vision system must inspect **120 frames/second** (latency < 8.33 ms). Real-time stream processing platforms like **Apache Kafka and Apache Flink** enable this.

**3. Variety (1.4 marks):**
Variety captures **diverse data formats**. Smart manufacturing integrates structured (ERP), semi-structured (JSON sensor logs), and unstructured (vibration waveforms, video) data, requiring **polyglot persistence** with NoSQL databases.

**4. Veracity (1.4 marks):**
Veracity addresses **data trustworthiness**. Sensor drift in a CNC machine produces noisy data; **anomaly detection algorithms** and **sensor fusion techniques** enhance data quality, ensuring accurate predictive maintenance.

**5. Value (1.4 marks):**
Value is the **actionable business insight** derived. Predictive maintenance using Big Data analytics can deliver **15–25% reduction in maintenance costs** and **70–75% decrease in breakdowns**, contributing directly to **OEE improvement**.

#### Part (b) — Challenges and Technologies (7 marks)

| **Challenge (Big Data V)** | **Industrial Impact** | **Solution / Technology** |
|----------------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Volume (1.75 marks) | Storage capacity overflow | **HDFS**, **Amazon S3**, data compression |
| Velocity (1.75 marks) | Delayed decision making | **Apache Kafka**, **Spark Streaming**, Edge Computing |
| Variety (1.75 marks) | Integration complexity | **Data Lakes**, **ETL pipelines**, **NoSQL** (MongoDB, Cassandra) |
| Veracity + Value (1.75 marks) | Poor insights, low ROI | **ML-based anomaly detection**, **Digital Twins**, **Data Governance frameworks** |

A **Lambda Architecture** combining batch layer (Hadoop) and speed layer (Storm/Flink) provides a robust solution for Industry 4.0 Big Data pipelines.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Big Data processing pipeline** in an Industry 4.0 smart factory. **(7 marks)**
**b)** A smart manufacturing plant deploys **5,000 IoT sensors**, each producing **2 KB of data per second**, operating **16 hours/day**. HDFS uses a **replication factor of 2**. Calculate the **total weekly storage requirement in GB**, and comment on the significance of the **Veracity** characteristic for this scenario. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B:

#### Part (a) — Big Data Processing Pipeline (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Data Sources (2 marks):**
- **IoT Sensors:** Temperature, vibration, pressure, humidity
- **SCADA Systems:** Real-time machine control data
- **ERP / MES Platforms:** Production scheduling, inventory
- **M2M Communication:** Inter-device telemetry
- **CCTV / Vision Systems:** Video quality inspection

**Step 2 — Ingestion Layer (1 mark):**
Tools like **Apache Kafka** or **AWS Kinesis** collect high-velocity streams and queue them for processing.

**Step 3 — Distributed Storage (1 mark):**
**HDFS** or **Data Lakes** store petabyte-scale data with fault-tolerance via replication.

**Step 4 — Processing Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Batch processing** (Hadoop MapReduce) for historical analytics
- **Stream processing** (Apache Flink) for real-time decisions

**Step 5 — Analytics & Visualization (1.5 marks):**
**Machine Learning models** (predictive maintenance, anomaly detection) feed into **dashboards** (Grafana, Power BI) for operator decisions, completing a **closed feedback loop** to the shop floor.

#### Part (b) — Storage Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Total Data Rate (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{Total Rate} = 5{,}000 \times 2 \text{ KB/s} = 10{,}000 \text{ KB/s} = 10{,}000 \times 1{,}024 \text{ B/s} = 10{,}240{,}000 \text{ B/s}
$$

**Step 2: Operating Time per Week (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{Operating Time} = 16 \text{ hrs/day} \times 7 \text{ days} = 112 \text{ hours} = 112 \times 3{,}600 \text{ s} = 403{,}200 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Raw Weekly Data (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{Weekly Raw} = 10{,}240{,}000 \times 403{,}200 = 4.1287 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bytes} \approx 4{,}128.7 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4: With Replication Factor 2 (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{Total Storage} = 4{,}128.7 \text{ GB} \times 2 = 8{,}257.4 \text{ GB} \approx 8.06 \text{ TB/week}
$$

**Step 5: Significance of Veracity (1 mark)**
With 5,000 sensors, even a **1% sensor malfunction rate** produces **50 faulty data streams** that can mislead predictive maintenance algorithms, trigger false alarms, or miss genuine failures. Robust **data validation, sensor calibration, and ML-based outlier detection** are essential to maintain **Veracity**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Volume with Velocity** — Volume is about *size* (TB/PB), Velocity is about *speed* (ms/eps).
> 2. **Always mention Industry 4.0 examples** specifically — generic examples (like "social media") will receive only partial credit.
> 3. **Always include units in numerical answers** — marks are deducted for "8 TB/week" without showing the calculation path.
> 4. **Draw the diagram** for Question B(a) — a missing pipeline diagram can cost up to **2 marks**.
> 5. **Veracity is often overlooked** by students — ensure you address it explicitly when discussing data quality challenges.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of Big Data**

- **Core Framework:** Big Data is characterized by the **5 V's** — **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value**.
- **Volume:** Measured in **Petabytes (10¹⁵ B) → Zettabytes (10²¹ B)**. Industry 4.0 sources: IoT, smart factory logs, connected vehicles.
- **Velocity:** Two types — **Batch** and **Real-Time Stream**. Real-time latency often in **milliseconds** (e.g., autonomous vehicles < 100 ms).
- **Variety:** Three sub-types — **Structured (~10–20%)**, **Semi-Structured**, and **Unstructured (~80–90%)**. Unstructured includes video, audio, sensor waveforms.
- **Veracity:** Refers to **trustworthiness and data quality**. Faulty sensors can cascade into wrong industrial decisions.
- **Value:** Data has **low intrinsic value**; insights derived from analytics create the **business ROI**.
- **Tools & Technologies:** **Hadoop HDFS** (storage), **Apache Kafka/Flink** (velocity), **NoSQL/Data Lakes** (variety), **ML algorithms** (veracity), **Digital Twins** (value).
- **Key Industry 4.0 Use Cases:** **Predictive Maintenance**, **Digital Twin Simulation**, **Smart Quality Inspection**, **Supply Chain 4.0 Optimization**.
- **Standard Data Units:** MB → GB → TB → PB → EB → ZB → YB (each step = 1024× previous in binary or 1000× in SI).
- **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer with **definition + Industry 4.0 example + technology/justification** for each characteristic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industry 4.0** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, **Big Data Sources** refer to the diverse origins, channels, and systems that generate, capture, transmit, or store massive volumes of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data at extremely high velocity and in a wide variety of formats. These sources are the foundational feeders of the Big Data ecosystem that fuels smart factories, cyber-physical systems, IoT platforms, predictive analytics, and AI-driven decision-making pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Focus):**
> Big Data Sources are the **data-generating entities** (machines, humans, sensors, enterprises, social platforms, and external systems) whose continuous and voluminous data emission creates the raw input for Big Data analytics, Hadoop-style distributed storage, and Industry 4.0 intelligent applications.

## 1.2 The Three Universal Categories of Big Data Sources

According to standard data engineering taxonomy (which is what KTU examiners expect), Big Data Sources are classified into **three universal categories**:

| S.No | Source Category | Origin Type | Industry 4.0 Relevance |
|------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Machine-Generated Data** | Sensors, machines, devices, IoT nodes | Smart factory floor, predictive maintenance |
| 2 | **Human-Generated Data** | People using digital platforms, social media, devices | Customer behaviour, social listening |
| 3 | **Business/Organization-Generated Data** | Enterprise systems, transactions, internal records | ERP, CRM, supply chain analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module explicitly emphasizes that in an Industry 4.0 environment, **Machine-Generated Data (IoT and CPS)** forms the **largest and fastest-growing** Big Data source, because every sensor, actuator, RFID tag, and PLC on the shop floor continuously emits telemetry data.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Water Reservoir" Model

Imagine a massive city reservoir that supplies water to millions of homes, factories, and farms. The reservoir does not care whether the water came from a mountain spring, a river, a desalination plant, or a household well — it just needs to handle the **incoming volume, variety, and speed**.

- The **mountain spring** is like **Machine-Generated Data** (sensors continuously push readings like temperature, vibration, pressure).
- The **household well** is like **Human-Generated Data** (people post tweets, upload videos, type searches).
- The **city pipeline** is like **Business-Generated Data** (the company's ERP, billing, and inventory systems push transaction records).

The reservoir (your Big Data platform — Hadoop, Spark, or cloud data lake) does not discriminate; it absorbs everything. **Big Data Sources** are simply the *faucets* and *feeders* of this reservoir. Without identifying and understanding these sources, an Industry 4.0 engineer cannot design a meaningful data pipeline.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** Think **"M-H-B"** → **M**achine, **H**uman, **B**usiness. This is the order in which KTU examiners expect the sources to be listed in 14-mark answers.

## 1.4 Key Metrics Used to Describe Big Data Sources

To describe a Big Data Source rigorously, engineers use the **5V model** (sometimes extended to 7V in advanced KTU elective modules):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Five V's of Big Data** (the KTU-defining framework):
> 1. **Volume** — Terabytes to Petabytes per day.
> 2. **Velocity** — Millisecond to real-time streaming speed.
> 3. **Variety** — Structured, semi-structured, unstructured.
> 4. **Veracity** — Trustworthiness and quality of the data.
> 5. **Value** — Business insight extractable from the data.

**Standard units** you must memorize for the exam:

- **1 Kilobyte (KB)** = 10³ bytes
- **1 Megabyte (MB)** = 10⁶ bytes
- **1 Gigabyte (GB)** = 10⁹ bytes
- **1 Terabyte (TB)** = 10¹² bytes
- **1 Petabyte (PB)** = 10¹⁵ bytes
- **1 Exabyte (EB)** = 10¹⁸ bytes
- **1 Zettabyte (ZB)** = 10²¹ bytes

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Big Data Source Categories

### 2.1.1 Machine-Generated Data Sources (The Industrial Backbone)

**Definition:** Data produced autonomously by machines, sensors, actuators, and devices without any direct human intervention. This is the *core* source in Industry 4.0.

**Operational Logic (Why it matters):**

- Sensors sample physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, vibration, humidity) at fixed intervals.
- Each sample becomes a **data point** with a **timestamp**.
- Thousands of sensors on a shop floor collectively produce **terabytes per day**.
- The data is typically **time-series** in nature — perfect for predictive maintenance and digital twins.

**Sub-Categories of Machine-Generated Data:**

- **IoT Sensor Data** — temperature, humidity, motion, light, gas.
- **Industrial Equipment Logs** — SCADA, PLC, CNC machine logs.
- **GPS / Telematics Data** — fleet vehicles, drones, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles).
- **Satellite & Geospatial Imagery** — remote sensing for agriculture and mining.
- **Network Logs & Server Telemetry** — syslog, SNMP traps, application logs.
- **Medical Device Streams** — patient monitors, MRI, infusion pumps.
- **Smart Meter / Smart Grid Data** — electricity, water, gas consumption.

### 2.1.2 Human-Generated Data Sources (The Social & Behavioural Layer)

**Definition:** Data created directly or indirectly by people interacting with digital systems, networks, and platforms.

**Operational Logic (How it forms):**

- Every click, like, share, search query, photo upload, or email becomes a data record.
- Social media platforms (Facebook, X, Instagram, LinkedIn) generate **petabytes per day** of user-generated content.
- Mobile apps continuously capture **location, accelerometer, gyroscope** readings from smartphones.

**Sub-Categories of Human-Generated Data:**

- **Social Media Posts** — text, images, video, comments.
- **Email & Messaging Logs** — corporate and personal communication archives.
- **Web Clickstreams** — browsing behaviour, search queries.
- **Multimedia Content** — YouTube videos, Instagram reels, podcasts.
- **Health & Fitness Data** — wearables like smartwatches, fitness bands.
- **E-commerce Behaviour** — purchases, cart additions, wishlists, reviews.

### 2.1.3 Business / Organization-Generated Data Sources (The Enterprise Core)

**Definition:** Data created as a byproduct of normal business operations, transactions, and internal enterprise processes.

**Operational Logic (Why it is structured):**

- Generated by enterprise software like **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)**, **CRM (Customer Relationship Management)**, **SCM (Supply Chain Management)**, and **HRMS (Human Resource Management Systems)**.
- Highly structured, transactional, and easy to query using SQL.
- Forms the "official record" of business activity — invoices, purchase orders, payroll, inventory.

**Sub-Categories of Business-Generated Data:**

- **Transactional Data** — sales, purchases, billing, payments.
- **Database Records** — MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL operational databases.
- **Financial Records** — ledgers, balance sheets, tax filings.
- **Customer Data** — CRM profiles, support tickets, complaint logs.
- **Supply Chain Data** — shipments, warehouse inventory, supplier performance.

## 2.2 The 5V Framework Applied to Source Identification

When a KTU question asks you to *classify* a Big Data source, use the **5V lens**:

| V-Factor | Question You Ask the Source | Example Answer |
|----------|------------------------------|----------------|
| **Volume** | How much data per day? | 500 GB/day from CNC machines |
| **Velocity** | How fast is it generated? | 1000 readings/sec from vibration sensors |
| **Variety** | What format is it in? | JSON, CSV, MP4, free text |
| **Veracity** | How trustworthy is it? | Sensor calibration drift = lower veracity |
| **Value** | What insight can I extract? | Predict bearing failure 7 days in advance |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Big Data Sources. Memorize the units and conversions — they appear in numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Data Volume Generated | $V = N \times S \times f$ | Bytes/day | N = number of sensors, S = sample size (bytes), f = sampling frequency (Hz) |
| 2 | Total Daily Volume (multiple sources) | $V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$ | Bytes/day | Sum across all source streams |
| 3 | Velocity (throughput) | $v = \frac{D}{t}$ | Bytes/sec | D = data amount, t = time window |
| 4 | Storage Conversion | $1 \text{ PB} = 10^{6} \text{ GB} = 10^{9} \text{ MB}$ | — | Standard SI-like decimal conversion |
| 5 | Compression Ratio | $C_r = \frac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}}$ | Dimensionless | E.g., 4:1 compression typical for sensor logs |
| 6 | Data Growth Rate (CAGR) | $V_{n} = V_{0} \cdot (1 + r)^{n}$ | Bytes | r = annual growth rate, n = years |
| 7 | Effective Throughput after compression | $V_{eff} = \frac{V}{C_r}$ | Bytes | Storage requirement after compression |
| 8 | Annual Volume | $V_{year} = V_{daily} \times 365$ | Bytes/year | For capacity planning |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar `\|` symbol is intentionally avoided in formulas above. For absolute value or "such that" notation, KTU examiners accept the $\vert$ symbol rendered through LaTeX.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of Big Data Sources directly enables:

- **Smart Factory Design (Industry 4.0)** — knowing *which* machine generates *which* data type helps design the right storage tier (hot vs. cold) and the right processing engine (streaming vs. batch).
- **Cybersecurity Monitoring** — network log sources feed SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) systems.
- **Predictive Maintenance** — vibration + temperature + acoustic emission sensor data sources feed ML models to predict equipment failure.
- **Supply Chain Optimization** — RFID + GPS + ERP transaction sources together enable end-to-end visibility.
- **Healthcare Analytics** — EHR (Electronic Health Record) + wearable + imaging sources feed clinical decision support systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Daily Data Volume from an IoT Sensor Array

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A smart factory floor has **250 IoT sensors**. Each sensor samples **6 physical parameters** (temperature, pressure, vibration, humidity, current, voltage). Each sample is stored as a **4-byte floating-point number**. The sampling rate is **50 Hz**. Calculate the **total daily data volume** generated. Express your answer in **GB/day**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

- Number of sensors: $N = 250$
- Parameters per sensor: $P = 6$
- Sample size: $S = 4 \text{ bytes}$
- Sampling frequency: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (samples per second per parameter)
- Time in one day: $T = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86400 \text{ seconds}$

**Step 2 — Compute samples generated per second by a single sensor.**

$$
\text{Samples per sensor per second} = P \times f = 6 \times 50 = 300 \text{ samples/sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute samples per second across all 250 sensors.**

$$
\text{Total samples per second} = N \times P \times f = 250 \times 6 \times 50
$$

$$
\text{Total samples per second} = 250 \times 300 = 75000 \text{ samples/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total samples in one day.**

$$
\text{Total samples per day} = 75000 \times 86400
$$

$$
\text{Total samples per day} = 6.48 \times 10^{9} \text{ samples/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total bytes per day.**

$$
V_{daily} = \text{Total samples} \times S = 6.48 \times 10^{9} \times 4
$$

$$
V_{daily} = 25.92 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes/day} = 2.592 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert bytes to Gigabytes.**

$$
V_{daily} = \frac{2.592 \times 10^{10}}{10^{9}} = 25.92 \text{ GB/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The smart factory generates approximately **25.92 GB of raw sensor data per day** from its 250-sensor array.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Storage Requirement with Compression

**Problem Statement:**
A retail e-commerce platform generates **3 TB/day** of business data (transactions, clickstreams, customer logs). If the company applies a **compression ratio of 4:1** and retains data for **5 years**, calculate the **total compressed storage requirement in Petabytes (PB)**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily volume.**

$$
V_{daily} = 3 \text{ TB/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual volume before compression.**

$$
V_{year} = 3 \times 365 = 1095 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Total 5-year volume before compression.**

$$
V_{5y} = 1095 \times 5 = 5475 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply compression ratio $C_r = 4$.**

$$
V_{compressed} = \frac{5475}{4} = 1368.75 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert TB to PB.**

$$
V_{compressed} = \frac{1368.75}{1000} = 1.36875 \text{ PB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Total compressed storage for 5 years ≈ **1.37 PB**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CAGR-Based Forecast of Data Growth

**Problem Statement:**
A hospital's EHR (Electronic Health Record) system currently holds **2 PB** of patient data. Data is growing at an annual rate of **35%** (CAGR). Predict the volume after **4 years**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the CAGR formula.**

$$
V_{n} = V_{0} \cdot (1 + r)^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
V_{4} = 2 \times (1 + 0.35)^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(1.35)^4$ step by step.**

$$
(1.35)^2 = 1.8225
$$

$$
(1.35)^4 = (1.8225)^2 = 3.32150625
$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication.**

$$
V_{4} = 2 \times 3.32150625 = 6.6430125 \text{ PB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** After 4 years, the hospital's EHR system will hold approximately **6.64 PB** of data — more than tripling in size.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Source-to-Application Mapping

| Source Category | Data Type | Typical Format | Storage Tier | Industry 4.0 Application | Veracity Concern |
|------------------|-----------|----------------|--------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| IoT sensors | Time-series | CSV, JSON | Hot SSD | Predictive maintenance | Sensor drift |
| CCTV cameras | Video stream | MP4, H.264 | Warm object store | Safety compliance | Low (always real) |
| ERP transactions | Structured | SQL tables | Hot RDBMS | Supply chain | High (audited) |
| Social media | Unstructured text + media | JSON, XML | Cold data lake | Brand sentiment | Low (noisy) |
| Web clickstreams | Semi-structured | JSON logs | Warm NoSQL | Conversion optimization | Medium |
| Email archives | Unstructured | PST, MBOX | Cold archive | Compliance/legal | High (encrypted) |
| Medical wearables | Time-series | HL7, FHIR | Hot encrypted | Patient monitoring | Medium (device variance) |
| Smart meters | Time-series | CSV, MQTT | Warm time-series DB | Demand forecasting | High (calibrated) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Big Data Sources. List any four major categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Big Data Sources are the systems, devices, platforms, or processes that generate, capture, or transmit massive volumes of data characterized by high Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value. In an Industry 4.0 context, these sources form the raw input layer for analytics and intelligent decision-making.

**Four major categories:**

1. **Machine-Generated Data** — Example: IoT temperature sensors on a shop floor.
2. **Human-Generated Data** — Example: Twitter posts about a product.
3. **Business-Generated Data** — Example: Sales transactions from an ERP system.
4. **External/Open-Source Data** — Example: Public weather data from a government API.

*(Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four categories with examples.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Machine-Generated Data and Human-Generated Data in the context of Industry 4.0.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Machine-Generated Data | Human-Generated Data |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Origin | Sensors, IoT devices, PLCs, SCADA systems | Social media, emails, clicks, user uploads |
| Volume | Very high and predictable (TB–PB/day) | Extremely high but bursty |
| Structure | Mostly structured or semi-structured | Mostly unstructured (text, image, video) |
| Velocity | Continuous, real-time streaming | Event-driven, asynchronous |
| Industry 4.0 Use | Predictive maintenance, digital twin | Customer sentiment, demand sensing |

*(Distribution: 1 mark for conceptual difference + 2 marks for tabular comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and are typically split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two full-length answer choices are provided below for thorough practice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a) Explain in detail the three universal categories of Big Data Sources with suitable Industry 4.0 examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three universal categories of Big Data Sources are:

**1. Machine-Generated Data (3 marks):**
This is data produced autonomously by machines, sensors, and devices without human intervention. In an Industry 4.0 smart factory, IoT temperature sensors, vibration sensors on motors, PLCs controlling assembly lines, and CNC machines all continuously emit telemetry. A single connected car can produce **4 TB of data per day**. The data is high-velocity, time-series, and primarily used for predictive maintenance, digital twin synchronization, and quality control. Other examples include smart grid meters, medical patient monitors, and satellite imagery.

**2. Human-Generated Data (2 marks):**
This is data created by human interaction with digital systems. Social media posts on platforms like Facebook and X, YouTube video uploads, Google search queries, WhatsApp messages, and wearable fitness data all qualify. In Industry 4.0, this data is invaluable for **demand forecasting**, **brand sentiment analysis**, and **customer experience optimization**. A retail company can mine Twitter to detect viral fashion trends and adjust production.

**3. Business/Organization-Generated Data (2 marks):**
This is data produced as a byproduct of enterprise operations. Examples include ERP transaction logs, CRM customer profiles, supply chain shipment records, financial ledgers, and HR payroll systems. It is highly structured, SQL-queryable, and forms the **"single source of truth"** for enterprise reporting. In Industry 4.0, this data is integrated with IoT streams for end-to-end supply chain visibility.

---

**(b) Describe the 5V characteristics of Big Data. Apply each V to an Industry 4.0 sensor source. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 5V framework characterizes Big Data sources. Applying it to a **vibration sensor on a CNC spindle**:

**1. Volume (1.5 marks):**
A single vibration sensor sampling at 10 kHz with 4-byte floats generates:

$$
V = 10000 \times 4 = 40000 \text{ bytes/sec} \approx 3.4 \text{ GB/day per sensor}
$$

A factory with 500 such sensors produces **~1.7 TB/day** of vibration data alone.

**2. Velocity (1.5 marks):**
Data is generated in real-time at 10,000 samples/second, requiring streaming engines like **Apache Kafka** or **Flink** for inline processing to detect anomalies.

**3. Variety (1 mark):**
Data is semi-structured (CSV, JSON, Parquet), but coexists with unstructured data like operator notes and structured data from ERP systems, forming a heterogeneous data lake.

**4. Veracity (1.5 marks):**
Sensor calibration drift, electromagnetic interference, and mounting looseness can introduce noise. Veracity is maintained through calibration logs, redundant sensors, and signal-quality flags.

**5. Value (1.5 marks):**
The true value is **predictive** — by applying FFT and ML models to vibration spectra, bearing failures can be predicted **7–14 days in advance**, saving millions in unplanned downtime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) A smart manufacturing plant deploys 400 IoT sensors. Each sensor records 8 parameters as 8-byte doubles at 100 Hz sampling rate. Calculate the daily data volume in GB. If 5:1 compression is applied, what is the effective storage per year in TB? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

- $N = 400$ sensors
- $P = 8$ parameters
- $S = 8$ bytes per sample
- $f = 100$ Hz
- $T = 86400$ seconds/day

**Step 2 — Compute total samples per day.**

$$
\text{Total samples} = N \times P \times f \times T
$$

$$
= 400 \times 8 \times 100 \times 86400
$$

$$
= 400 \times 8 \times 8.64 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
= 400 \times 6.912 \times 10^{7} = 2.7648 \times 10^{10} \text{ samples/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute daily bytes.**

$$
V_{daily} = 2.7648 \times 10^{10} \times 8 = 2.21184 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to GB.**

$$
V_{daily} = \frac{2.21184 \times 10^{11}}{10^{9}} = 221.184 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 5:1 compression.**

$$
V_{compressed\_daily} = \frac{221.184}{5} = 44.237 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual compressed volume.**

$$
V_{year} = 44.237 \times 365 = 16146.5 \text{ GB/year} \approx 16.15 \text{ TB/year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Daily raw volume = **221.18 GB/day**; Compressed annual storage ≈ **16.15 TB/year**.

---

**(b) Discuss the role of Big Data Sources in enabling Industry 4.0 applications. Give four application examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Big Data Sources are the **fuel** of Industry 4.0. Without diverse, high-velocity, high-volume sources, the smart factory cannot function. Their role is:

**1. Enabling Digital Twins (2 marks):**
Real-time sensor data (temperature, vibration, pressure) feeds the digital replica of a physical asset, allowing operators to simulate scenarios and predict behaviour without touching the actual machine.

**2. Predictive Maintenance (2 marks):**
Vibration, acoustic, and thermal sensor sources feed ML models that forecast equipment failure. This reduces unplanned downtime by up to **50%** and cuts maintenance costs by **10–25%**.

**3. Smart Supply Chain (1.5 marks):**
RFID tags, GPS telematics, ERP transactions, and supplier portals together provide end-to-end visibility. Disruptions can be detected and rerouted in real time.

**4. Quality Control using Computer Vision (1.5 marks):**
High-resolution cameras on assembly lines capture millions of images per day. CNN-based defect detection models classify products in milliseconds, replacing manual inspection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up categories:** Many students write "Big Data Sources" as just "social media" — you *must* cover all three (Machine, Human, Business) for full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Numerical problems that end with "in GB" or "in TB" lose 1 mark if units are omitted.
> 3. **Skipping the 5V mapping:** A 14-mark answer on Big Data Sources that does not link the source to the 5V framework is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 4. **Ignoring Industry 4.0 context:** KTU explicitly tests Industry 4.0 relevance. Always tie your answer back to smart factory / IoT / digital twin.
> 5. **Formula errors in CAGR:** Use parentheses correctly: $(1 + r)^n$, not $1 + r^n$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for last-night revision):**

- ✅ Big Data Sources = origins of massive, high-velocity, varied data streams.
- ✅ **Three universal categories:** Machine-Generated, Human-Generated, Business/Organization-Generated. Mnemonic: **"M-H-B"**.
- ✅ Machine-Generated is the **largest** source in Industry 4.0 (IoT, PLC, SCADA, sensors).
- ✅ Human-Generated includes social media, emails, clickstreams, wearable data.
- ✅ Business-Generated includes ERP, CRM, transactional, financial, supply chain data.
- ✅ The **5V model** is mandatory in every descriptive answer: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ✅ Standard volume units: KB (10³) → MB (10⁶) → GB (10⁹) → TB (10¹²) → PB (10¹⁵) → EB (10¹⁸).
- ✅ Core formula: $V_{daily} = N \times P \times S \times f \times 86400$ bytes.
- ✅ CAGR formula: $V_n = V_0 \times (1 + r)^n$.
- ✅ Compression effective volume: $V_{eff} = \frac{V_{original}}{C_r}$.
- ✅ Annual volume = $V_{daily} \times 365$.
- ✅ Industry 4.0 applications: Digital Twin, Predictive Maintenance, Smart Supply Chain, Computer Vision QC.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, always structure: *Definition → Categories with examples → 5V mapping → Industry 4.0 application → Numerical example if asked*.
- ✅ Common traps: unit omission, missing 5V, no Industry 4.0 linkage, wrong formula placement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Acquisition and Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET862 - INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Big Data Acquisition and Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Big Data Acquisition** is the systematic process of identifying, capturing, ingesting, and transporting massive volumes of heterogeneous data from distributed sources (sensors, machines, social platforms, transactional systems, logs) into a centralized or distributed storage environment where it can be processed, analyzed, and used for decision-making.

**Big Data Storage** refers to the architectural framework, technologies, and methodologies used to persist, manage, retrieve, and protect extremely large, fast-moving, and structurally diverse datasets across distributed computing infrastructure (on-premise clusters, cloud, or hybrid systems).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Definition: Big Data is characterized by the **5 V Framework** — **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value** — and its acquisition/storage pipeline forms the foundational Layer-1 of any Industry 4.0 Cyber-Physical System (CPS) architecture.

## 1.2 The 5 V's of Big Data (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

| Characteristic | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|----------------|------------|---------------------------|
| **Volume** | Scale of data in Terabytes → Petabytes → Zettabytes | Determines storage sizing |
| **Velocity** | Speed at which data is generated and processed (batch/real-time) | Determines ingestion architecture |
| **Variety** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured formats | Determines schema flexibility |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness, quality, noise of data | Determines cleansing pipeline |
| **Value** | Business/operational insight extracted | Determines ROI of system |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Library" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine Big Data as a **Smart City Public Library System**:
> - **Acquisition** = Trucks (ingestion pipelines) constantly arriving from hundreds of publishers, newsrooms, sensors, and citizens — every minute, every second.
> - **Storage** = The library warehouse with millions of shelves, sorted across multiple branches (distributed storage like HDFS), each holding duplicate copies in case one branch catches fire (replication factor).
> - **Variety** = Some books are hardcover (structured SQL rows), some are magazines (semi-structured JSON/XML), some are handwritten scrolls (unstructured images, video).
> - **Velocity** = Reference books are delivered weekly (batch ingestion), but breaking-news telegrams arrive every second (stream ingestion via Apache Kafka).
> - **Value** = A librarian who turns this chaos into curated knowledge (analytics & AI).

Just as a city cannot function without organized libraries, a smart factory (Industry 4.0) cannot operate without robust Big Data acquisition and storage layers.

## 1.4 Sources of Big Data in Industry 4.0 Context

- **Machine/Operational Data**: PLCs, SCADA, CNC machines, IoT sensors on shop floor
- **Enterprise Data**: ERP (SAP), MES, CRM, supply chain transactions
- **Human-Generated Data**: Operator logs, social media feedback, emails
- **External Data**: Weather APIs, market prices, regulatory feeds
- **Video/Image Data**: CCTV, quality inspection cameras, thermal imaging
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Big Data Acquisition Pipeline — Operational Breakdown

The acquisition pipeline consists of **four sequential stages**, each with distinct engineering responsibilities:

### Stage 1 — Data Source Identification
- Catalog all internal (machines, ERP) and external (cloud APIs, social) data generators
- Tag each source by its **velocity class** (batch, micro-batch, streaming) and **format class** (CSV, JSON, Parquet, Avro, binary)

### Stage 2 — Data Ingestion
Two primary paradigms:
- **Batch Ingestion**: Data accumulated over time-windows (hourly/daily), transferred via scheduled jobs (e.g., Apache Sqoop, AWS Glue). Used for historical ETL workloads.
- **Stream Ingestion**: Real-time event-driven capture (e.g., Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis, Azure Event Hubs). Used for sensor telemetry, fraud detection, predictive maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU High-Yield Point: A hybrid **Lambda Architecture** combines batch layer (for accuracy) + speed layer (for low-latency) + serving layer (for query merging) — frequently asked in 14-mark questions.

### Stage 3 — Data Pre-processing & Validation
- Schema validation, deduplication, timestamp normalization
- Encryption-in-transit (TLS/SSL), authentication (Kerberos, OAuth)
- Quality scoring before landing into storage

### Stage 4 — Data Landing into Storage
- Data lands in raw zone → cleaned zone → curated zone (Medallion Architecture)
- Format conversion to columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) for analytical efficiency

## 2.2 Big Data Storage Architectures — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Distributed File Systems — HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)
- **Master-Slave** architecture: 1 NameNode (metadata manager) + N DataNodes (actual storage)
- Default **Replication Factor = 3** (2 in same rack, 1 in different rack — rack-aware placement)
- Block size default: **128 MB** (vs 4 KB in traditional file systems)
- Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM) access pattern — ideal for analytical workloads

### 2.2.2 NoSQL Databases (Not Only SQL)
Four families, each suited for different Industry 4.0 use cases:

| Type | Data Model | Industry 4.0 Use Case | Example |
|------|------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Key-Value** | Hash-map style (key → value) | Real-time sensor lookup, session caching | Redis, Amazon DynamoDB |
| **Document** | JSON / BSON documents | Equipment manuals, configuration logs | MongoDB, CouchDB |
| **Column-Family** | Column-oriented tables | Time-series analytics, IoT telemetry | Cassandra, HBase |
| **Graph** | Nodes + Edges | Digital twin relationship mapping | Neo4j, Amazon Neptune |

### 2.2.3 Data Lake vs Data Warehouse vs Data Lakehouse

| Feature | Data Warehouse | Data Lake | Data Lakehouse |
|---------|----------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Data Type** | Structured only | All (raw) | All (raw + curated) |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read | Schema-on-read + write |
| **Storage Cost** | High | Low | Low-Medium |
| **Best For** | BI reporting | ML, exploration | Unified BI + ML |
| **Example** | Snowflake, Redshift | AWS S3, Azure Data Lake | Databricks Delta Lake |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula tested in KTU ESE for Big Data sizing and storage estimation. No `|` (absolute value) notation is needed in this domain; absolute values are denoted via `\vert` when required.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|--------------|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Total Data Volume** | $V_{total} = N_{sources} \times R_{sampling} \times T$ | Total bytes captured from N sources over time T | Bytes (KB, MB, GB, TB, PB) |
| 2 | **Storage with Replication** | $S_{required} = V_{total} \times R_{replication}$ | Total cluster storage after HDFS-style replication | Bytes |
| 3 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}}$ | How many times smaller compressed data is | Unitless |
| 4 | **Effective Storage** | $S_{effective} = \dfrac{S_{required}}{CR}$ | Actual disk needed after compression | Bytes |
| 5 | **Ingestion Throughput** | $TP = \dfrac{V_{total}}{T_{transfer}}$ | Data ingestion rate | Bytes/second |
| 6 | **Storage Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{usable}}{S_{allocated}} \times 100\%$ | Useful storage as % of raw disk allocated | Percent |
| 7 | **Rack-Aware Distribution** | $R_{cross} \geq 1$, $R_{local} = R_{total} - R_{cross}$ | Replication split across racks | Integer count |
| 8 | **Scale-Out Capacity** | $S_{cluster} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{node,i}$ | Sum of individual node capacities | Bytes |
| 9 | **Annual Data Growth** | $V_{year} = V_{daily} \times 365 \times (1 + g)^{n}$ | Compounded annual storage growth (g = growth rate, n = years) | Bytes |
| 10 | **Time-to-Insight Latency** | $\tau_{insight} = \tau_{ingest} + \tau_{process} + \tau_{query}$ | End-to-end analytical latency | Seconds |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing**: A single automotive assembly line generates **~1 TB/day** of sensor + vision data; a Tier-1 OEM generates **~50 PB/year**
- **Predictive Maintenance**: Real-time stream ingestion of vibration data (10 kHz sampling) feeds ML models to forecast bearing failures 7–14 days in advance
- **Digital Twin Synchronization**: Graph databases (Neo4j) model complex equipment-bom-process relationships for real-time mirror simulation
- **Supply Chain Resilience**: Data Lake ingests weather + logistics + market feeds to optimize inventory dynamically

> [!NOTE]
> Industry 4.0 storage sizing typically follows: **Plan for 3× today's peak load within 2 years** (rule of thumb used by McKinsey and Gartner).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Smart Factory Storage Sizing (Industry 4.0 Case)

### Problem Statement

A CNC machining cell has the following deployment:
- **500 sensors** mounted on CNC machines, each sampling at **2 KB/s**
- Data is captured continuously for **24 hours/day**
- Data is stored in **HDFS** with default **Replication Factor (R) = 3**
- Parquet compression yields a **Compression Ratio (CR) = 4**
- Compute the following for **1 year** of operation:
  1. Daily raw data volume
  2. Daily replicated storage
  3. Daily effective compressed storage
  4. Annual effective storage
  5. Required ingestion throughput
  6. Cluster node count if each DataNode provides **20 TB raw** capacity

### Step-by-Step Derivation

#### Step 1 — Daily Raw Data Volume

$$
V_{daily} = N_{sources} \times R_{sampling} \times T_{per\,day}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_{daily} = 500 \times 2 \, \text{KB/s} \times 86400 \, \text{s/day}
$$

Multiplying sensor count and sampling rate first:

$$
500 \times 2 = 1000 \, \text{KB/s}
$$

Multiplying by seconds per day:

$$
V_{daily} = 1000 \, \text{KB/s} \times 86400 \, \text{s/day}
$$

$$
V_{daily} = 86{,}400{,}000 \, \text{KB/day}
$$

Converting KB → MB (divide by 1024):

$$
V_{daily} = \dfrac{86{,}400{,}000}{1024} \approx 84375 \, \text{MB/day}
$$

Converting MB → GB (divide by 1024):

$$
V_{daily} = \dfrac{84375}{1024} \approx 82.4 \, \text{GB/day}
$$

> ✅ **Result 1**: $V_{daily} \approx 82.4 \, \text{GB/day}$

#### Step 2 — Daily Replicated Storage

$$
S_{daily} = V_{daily} \times R_{replication}
$$

$$
S_{daily} = 82.4 \, \text{GB/day} \times 3
$$

$$
S_{daily} = 247.2 \, \text{GB/day}
$$

> ✅ **Result 2**: $S_{daily} = 247.2 \, \text{GB/day}$

#### Step 3 — Daily Effective Compressed Storage

$$
S_{effective,daily} = \dfrac{S_{daily}}{CR}
$$

$$
S_{effective,daily} = \dfrac{247.2}{4} = 61.8 \, \text{GB/day}
$$

> ✅ **Result 3**: $S_{effective,daily} = 61.8 \, \text{GB/day}$

#### Step 4 — Annual Effective Storage

$$
S_{annual} = S_{effective,daily} \times 365
$$

$$
S_{annual} = 61.8 \times 365
$$

$$
S_{annual} = 22557 \, \text{GB} \approx 22.56 \, \text{TB/year}
$$

> ✅ **Result 4**: $S_{annual} \approx 22.56 \, \text{TB/year}$

#### Step 5 — Required Ingestion Throughput

$$
TP = \dfrac{V_{daily}}{T_{transfer}}
$$

Assuming an **8-hour batch ingestion window**:

$$
T_{transfer} = 8 \times 3600 = 28800 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
TP = \dfrac{86.4 \times 1024 \times 1024 \, \text{KB}}{28800 \, \text{s}}
$$

Converting 82.4 GB → KB:

$$
V_{daily} = 82.4 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 86441984 \, \text{KB}
$$

$$
TP = \dfrac{86441984}{28800} \approx 3001 \, \text{KB/s} \approx 2.93 \, \text{MB/s}
$$

> ✅ **Result 5**: $TP \approx 2.93 \, \text{MB/s}$

#### Step 6 — Cluster DataNode Count

Each DataNode contributes 20 TB raw, but effective usable (after replication overhead $R=3$):

$$
C_{node,usable} = \dfrac{20 \, \text{TB}}{3} \approx 6.67 \, \text{TB usable/node}
$$

$$
N_{nodes} = \dfrac{S_{annual}}{C_{node,usable}}
$$

$$
N_{nodes} = \dfrac{22.56}{6.67} \approx 3.38
$$

Rounding up (cannot have fractional nodes):

$$
N_{nodes} = 4 \text{ DataNodes}
$$

> ✅ **Result 6**: Minimum **4 DataNodes** + 1 NameNode required

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Lambda Architecture Latency Calculation

### Problem Statement

A predictive maintenance system uses Lambda Architecture:
- Batch layer latency = **4 hours**
- Speed layer latency = **5 seconds**
- Serving layer merging latency = **0.5 seconds**

Compute total time-to-insight, and verify if it meets an SLA of **≤ 10 seconds** for real-time alerts.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The total time-to-insight is the sum of all three layers:

$$
\tau_{insight} = \tau_{ingest} + \tau_{process} + \tau_{query}
$$

For **real-time alert path** (using speed layer):

$$
\tau_{insight,real} = 5 + 0.5 = 5.5 \, \text{s}
$$

Since $5.5 \, \text{s} < 10 \, \text{s}$, the **SLA is met** for real-time alerts. ✅

For **batch reporting path** (using batch layer):

$$
\tau_{insight,batch} = 4 \times 3600 + 0.5 = 14400.5 \, \text{s} \approx 4 \, \text{hours}
$$

This is **NOT** acceptable for real-time SLA but acceptable for daily executive dashboards.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity Computed | Symbol | Final Value (Ex. 1) | Interpretation |
|-------------------|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Daily raw volume | $V_{daily}$ | 82.4 GB | Single-day capture |
| Replicated storage | $S_{daily}$ | 247.2 GB | HDFS overhead × 3 |
| Compressed storage | $S_{eff,daily}$ | 61.8 GB | After Parquet CR=4 |
| Annual storage | $S_{annual}$ | 22.56 TB | Year-1 footprint |
| Ingestion throughput | $TP$ | 2.93 MB/s | Bandwidth need |
| DataNode count | $N_{nodes}$ | 4 nodes | Cluster sizing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Big Data Acquisition-to-Storage End-to-End Pipeline

> **Diagram 1 Caption:** End-to-end flow of Industry 4.0 Big Data from heterogeneous sources through ingestion, validation, medallion zones, into multi-modal storage, and finally to analytics/AI consumers.

---

## 4.2 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture

```mermaid

```

> **Diagram 2 Caption:** HDFS architecture — NameNode stores metadata (file → block → DataNode mapping); DataNodes store 128 MB blocks with default 3× replication. Secondary NameNode assists in checkpointing (not a true hot standby).

---

## 4.3 NoSQL Database Family Taxonomy


> **Diagram 3 Caption:** NoSQL taxonomy — four families mapped to Industry 4.0 use cases. Key-Value → caching, Document → asset records, Column → IoT time-series, Graph → digital-twin relationships.

---

## 4.4 Medallion Storage Architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold)

```mermaid

dt1[DIGITAL TWIN]

src1 --> bronze
bronze --> silver
silver --> gold
gold --> bi1
gold --> ml1
```
